skill.name: product-manager-toolkit
skill.description: A product management toolkit for RICE prioritization, customer interview analysis, PRD drafting, discovery planning, and product strategy workflows.
skill.summary: Prioritize features, synthesize customer research, and draft product planning artifacts.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported critical and high risk patterns, but review found the severe signals are false positives from markdown templates, product abbreviations, and variable names. The real risk is limited to local CLI scripts that read user-selected files and create a fixed sample CSV file, with no evidence of network access, shell execution, credential access, or prompt injection.

low_findings.title: Markdown Examples Misclassified as External Commands
low_findings.description: The external command findings in SKILL.md are fenced usage examples that tell a user how to run the helper scripts. They are not executed by the skill itself, and the PRD template code fences contain product writing templates, not shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown code fences or templates. I found no subprocess, shell, eval, or command execution implementation in these markdown files.

low_findings.title: Sensitive, Crypto, Network, and Key Findings Are Naming Collisions
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM finding is the market sizing acronym in a PRD template, and weak crypto flags match ordinary product text such as descriptions and MVP-related template language. The network and certificate or key flags are also variable or method name matches, not HTTP clients or secrets handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited code and markdown use product management vocabulary, dictionary keys, and classification labels. I found no cryptographic API, credential file handling, HTTP import, or outbound network call.

low_findings.title: Local Filesystem Access in CLI Helpers
low_findings.description: The Python helpers read user-supplied local input files and can write a fixed sample CSV file in the current working directory. This is expected for command line utilities, but users should review input paths before running the scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The open calls are direct and easy to verify, but they operate only on local paths chosen by the invoking user. There is no evidence of path traversal exploitation, privilege escalation, or data exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Local File Paths
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI scripts accept file paths from command line arguments and read those local files. This is a common utility pattern, but it should be treated as local filesystem access when publishing the skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file reads are real and use CLI-provided paths. The risk remains low because execution is user initiated and there is no network transfer or hidden command execution.

content.user_title: Prioritize Product Work and Research Insights
content.value_statement: Product teams often struggle to connect research, prioritization, and planning artifacts. This skill provides repeatable workflows for RICE scoring, interview synthesis, PRDs, discovery, and roadmaps.
actual_capabilities: Calculates RICE scores from feature reach, impact, confidence, and effort inputs.
Generates portfolio summaries that separate quick wins, big bets, and roadmap capacity.
Analyzes interview transcripts for pain points, feature requests, sentiment, themes, and quotes.
Provides PRD, agile epic, one-page PRD, and feature brief templates.
Guides discovery workflows, metrics selection, stakeholder planning, and go-to-market preparation.
limitations: The interview analyzer uses keyword and pattern matching, not a trained research model.
RICE scores depend on the quality and consistency of the input estimates.
The scripts read local files and should only be run on trusted project data.
The toolkit does not integrate directly with PM tools, analytics tools, or issue trackers.

use_cases.title: Rank Feature Backlogs
use_cases.description: Score candidate features, compare effort against expected reach, and create a capacity-aware roadmap.

use_cases.title: Synthesize Customer Interviews
use_cases.description: Extract repeated pain points, requests, jobs to be done, sentiment, metrics, and useful quotes from transcripts.

use_cases.title: Draft Product Requirements
use_cases.description: Choose a PRD format and structure problem statements, scope, success metrics, risks, and acceptance criteria.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Feature Ranking
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prioritization from a small backlog.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the product-manager-toolkit to help me rank these features with RICE. Ask for any missing reach, impact, confidence, or effort values before scoring.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Interview Themes
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the customer interview workflow to summarize this transcript. Extract pain points, feature requests, jobs to be done, sentiment, metrics, and the strongest quotes.

prompt_templates.title: Create a PRD Draft
prompt_templates.scenario: Structured requirements writing for a scoped feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PRD templates to draft a product requirements document for this feature. Include problem, scope, non-goals, success metrics, risks, dependencies, and acceptance criteria.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Strategy Pack
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning across research, priority, and launch readiness.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the full toolkit to turn these research notes and feature ideas into a prioritized roadmap, PRD outline, discovery risks, stakeholder plan, and go-to-market checklist.

output_examples.input: A CSV of ten feature ideas with reach, impact, confidence, effort, and descriptions.
output_examples.output: A ranked feature list, RICE scores, portfolio balance notes, quick wins, big bets, and a quarter-by-quarter roadmap.

output_examples.input: A customer interview transcript about onboarding problems and reporting needs.
output_examples.output: A research summary with key pain points, requested improvements, sentiment, jobs to be done, recurring themes, metrics, and concise quotes.

output_examples.input: A product concept with target users, business goal, and known constraints.
output_examples.output: A PRD draft with problem definition, proposed solution, in-scope work, out-of-scope items, success metrics, risks, and open questions.

best_practices: Use consistent scoring definitions before comparing RICE scores across teams.
Combine interview analysis with human review before making roadmap decisions.
Keep PRDs focused on the problem, scope, measurable outcomes, and explicit non-goals.
anti_patterns: Do not treat keyword-based interview output as final research findings without validation.
Do not prioritize only by RICE score when strategic bets or dependencies matter.
Do not fill every PRD section when a lightweight feature brief would create faster alignment.

faq.question: What does this skill help product managers do?
faq.answer: It helps prioritize features, analyze customer interviews, draft PRDs, plan discovery, and structure product strategy decisions.

faq.question: Does it run scripts automatically?
faq.answer: No. The skill documents Python helper scripts that a user can run manually from the command line.

faq.question: Can it analyze customer interviews?
faq.answer: Yes. The included analyzer extracts pain points, requests, sentiment, jobs to be done, themes, metrics, and quotes.

faq.question: Can it replace product judgment?
faq.answer: No. It provides structure and calculations, but roadmap decisions still need context, strategy, and stakeholder review.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What data should users avoid sharing?
faq.answer: Users should avoid unredacted personal data, confidential customer details, and sensitive business information unless their environment allows it.
